title: The Bus Stop and the Bike Store
paras: The bus stop and the bike store. This is a practical, checklist-led guide to human-scale climate measures in everyday planning — the companion to "Planning for a Changing Climate". Where that article made the big argument, this one comes down to the scheme in front of you. It covers England, and it's written as at the 21st of July 2026.

Climate change is a vast problem, and planning's response is often discussed at a vast scale — net zero, strategic energy, the decarbonisation of whole cities. All of that matters. But it isn't where most planners spend their days. Most spend them on ordinary applications: a barn conversion, a small housing site, an extension, a change of use, a fifty-home scheme on the edge of a town. And it's precisely there, on the everyday application, that a development-management planner holds a set of small levers the strategic debate tends to overlook.

A bike store by the front door rather than behind the bins. A bus stop you can see from the houses. A roof left clear for solar. A front garden kept as garden, rather than paved for a car. None of these saves the planet on its own — but they're cumulative, they're within an ordinary planner's gift, and, placed well, they quietly shape how people live for the sixty-year life of the building. Get the bike store and the bus stop right, and you nudge a thousand daily journeys out of the car — every day, for decades. That's a small thing that adds up.

Two disciplines run through everything below. First, proportionality — scale the ask to the development. Demanding a car-club bay on a single dwelling brings the whole exercise into disrepute. And second, honesty about what planning can actually require — which is where we start.

title: 2. What planning can, and cannot, require
paras: So — what planning can, and cannot, require. A great deal of a building's climate performance is now set by the Building Regulations, not planning, and a planner who doesn't know the boundary will either ask for things they can't secure, or duplicate what the Regulations already deliver. Fabric efficiency, the heating system, ventilation, E-V charge points and overheating are matters for the Regulations. E-V charging in new homes with parking has been required under Part S since June 2022, and overheating mitigation under Part O since the same date.

The bigger shift is the Future Homes Standard. The Government published its response and the amended Building Regulations in March 2026; the standard comes into force on the 24th of March 2027, with a transition to 2028 — and it is a step change. New homes must emit at least seventy-five per cent less carbon than a 2013-standard home. They cannot use fossil-fuel heating — a heat pump or a heat-network connection, not a gas boiler. And they must include on-site renewable generation — in practice, solar over much of the roof. The consequence for planners is this: from 2027 the new home is low-carbon by default, so your attention shifts to what the Regulations do not cover. How people move. How the place is shaped. Greening, water, waste storage — and the layout that makes the low-carbon choice the easy one.

One significant exception to "leave energy to the Regulations" is worth knowing, because it's often mis-stated. A December 2023 Written Ministerial Statement discouraged local plans from setting energy standards above the Regulations. But in July 2025, the Court of Appeal confirmed that the Statement does not have the force of law, and does not override authorities' statutory power, under the Planning and Energy Act 2008, to set higher local energy standards where justified by local evidence. That's R, on the application of Rights: Community: Action, against the Secretary of State, reported at 2025, E-W-C-A Civ 990. So a sound, evidenced local policy can still ask for more than the national baseline. For the everyday development-management planner, though, the rule holds: don't gold-plate the Regulations case by case, and put your energy into the human-scale measures planning genuinely owns.

title: 3. Getting there without a car
paras: Now, getting there without a car. This is the single most valuable area a planner controls, because how a development connects to the world around it locks in travel behaviour for the life of the scheme — and transport is one of the hardest parts of the economy to decarbonise. The prize is not banning cars. It's making the walk, the cycle and the bus the easy, obvious choice — and small design decisions decide whether they are. On major applications, Active Travel England is now a statutory consultee, which gives active-travel provision real weight.

So, the checklist. First, walking: direct, safe, overlooked routes to where people actually go — shops, school, stop, centre — with desire lines followed, not fought. Second, crossings: safe, convenient crossings where routes meet roads, and no severance that pushes people back into the car. Third, cycle parking: secure, covered, accessible storage by the front door — not behind the bins — to L-T-N 1 of 20 standard, with room for cargo bikes. Fourth, the bus: a stop within an easy walk, with shelter, a seat and real-time information — and bus priority, or a service contribution, where the scheme can support one.

Fifth, connections: genuine links into the existing walking and cycling network — permeable for people, not a cul-de-sac island. Sixth, E-V and car clubs: E-V charging under Part S provided, and a car-club bay on larger schemes, to cut the need to own a car at all. And seventh, layout: front doors onto streets, active frontages, and low speeds by design — because design, not signage, changes behaviour.

title: 4. The building and its energy
paras: Next, the building and its energy. From 2027 the Future Homes Standard does much of this for you, so the planner's role is smaller than people assume — but it's not nothing, and it matters most on the schemes the Standard doesn't reach: conversions, changes of use, and everything built before it bites. Focus on what planning shapes. Orientation and form that work with sun and daylight. Roofs kept clear, and structurally ready, for solar. Space and routes for a heat pump. Connection to a heat network where one exists. And, above all, the measure with the largest carbon saving — keeping and reusing the existing building rather than demolishing it. Because the greenest building is the one that already exists.

The checks here. First, reuse first: has genuine retention and reuse of the existing building and its materials been explored, before demolition is accepted? Second, orientation: does the layout let homes benefit from daylight and passive solar, without designing in overheating — the Part O issue? Third, solar-ready roofs: are roofs oriented, unshaded and structurally able to take P-V — installed now, or ready for it?

Fourth, heat pumps: is there sensible, well-designed space for air-source heat pumps and their external units, considering noise and appearance? Fifth, heat networks: where a district network exists or is planned, has connection been secured or safeguarded? And sixth, no gold-plating: are energy asks either within an evidenced local policy, or left to the Regulations — not invented case by case?

title: 5. Water
paras: Five: water. Water is the quiet climate issue — parts of England are seriously water-stressed, and a hotter climate makes both drought and flash flooding worse. New homes are built to a baseline water-efficiency standard. But where a local plan is justified, an authority can require the tighter optional standard — a hundred and ten litres per person per day — by condition. And sustainable drainage does double duty: managing flood risk and, through planting, cooling and biodiversity.

The checklist. First, efficiency: where justified locally, the hundred-and-ten-litres-per-person-per-day optional standard, secured by condition. Second, sustainable drainage: SuDS designed in from the start — swales, basins, rain gardens — that hold and slow water rather than pipe it away. Third, permeable surfaces: permeable materials for drives, paths and parking, and resist the sealing of gardens and forecourts under impermeable hardstanding. Fourth, rainwater: water butts as standard, and rainwater harvesting on larger or non-residential schemes. And fifth, planting: drought-resilient, low-water planting that survives hotter, drier summers without irrigation.

title: 6. Waste and the circular economy
paras: Six: waste and the circular economy. Waste storage is the most neglected corner of a housing layout, and one of the easiest to get right — and it has a real behavioural effect. If recycling and food-waste bins are hard to reach, badly sited or too small, people do not use them. Designing storage in properly — accessible, adequate, and no further from the door than the general-waste bin — quietly lifts recycling rates for the life of the scheme. And with food-waste collections rolling out across England, adequate, well-placed storage is no longer optional.

The checklist. First, designed-in storage: adequate, accessible storage for general, recycling and food waste — convenient, or it will not be used. Second, food and compost: food-waste storage provided, and space for home composting on schemes with gardens. Third, reuse of buildings: existing buildings retained and reused where possible, saving their embodied carbon. Fourth, reuse of materials: reclaimed and low-embodied-carbon materials encouraged, and demolition arisings reused on site where practical. And fifth, construction waste: a site waste and demolition approach that designs out waste, rather than skipping it.

title: 7. Greening and cooling
paras: Seven: greening and cooling. Green infrastructure is the best-evidenced adaptation measure there is, and much of it is delivered at exactly the human scale this article is about — a tree in the right place, a hedge instead of a fence, a green roof, a front garden kept green. Trees shade and cool streets that will otherwise bake. Planting slows water. And since 2024, most development must deliver a ten per cent biodiversity net gain — major development from February 2024, small sites from April 2024 — which planning secures and, crucially, must see maintained. The commonest failure is not the requirement but the follow-through — the tree that dies unwatered, the net gain unmanaged after year two.

The checklist. First, trees for shade: new and retained trees where they shade streets, homes and hard surfaces — secured with the space, the soil and, above all, the maintenance to survive. Second, protect what exists: existing mature trees and hedgerows retained — a semi-mature tree cannot be replaced by a sapling for thirty years. Third, biodiversity net gain: the ten per cent delivered well, genuinely additional, and — the hard part — secured and managed long-term, not just drawn on a plan.

Fourth, green roofs and walls: green or brown roofs where they earn their place, for cooling, water and biodiversity. Fifth, gardens over paving: real gardens and permeable planting resisted from being paved over for parking, and front gardens kept green. And sixth, greening the streets: verges, street trees, rain gardens and hedgerows built in — not value-engineered out.

title: 8. Designing for a changing climate
paras: Eight: designing for a changing climate. Adaptation is the half of climate action planning has quietly always done. At the human scale, it's about making sure the homes and places built today will still be comfortable and safe in the hotter, wetter climate they will live through. Much of it costs little if designed in early — and a great deal if retrofitted later.

Four checks. First, overheating: orientation, shading, ventilation and glazing that keep homes cool in summer — Part O covers new homes, but this is a live issue for conversions and the existing stock. Second, flood resilience: development steered away from flood risk, and where it must go there, made safe — raised levels, resilient materials, safe access. Third, shade and shelter: trees, canopies and built shade in public spaces, streets and play areas, for a hotter climate. And fourth, future-ready: homes and layouts that can adapt — provision for future solar, E-V charging, heat pumps and cooling, without tearing the place up.

title: 9. Carbon, counted honestly
paras: Nine: carbon, counted honestly. A word on carbon and offsetting, because it's where good intentions most often slide into greenwash. The discipline is a hierarchy, and the order matters: reduce first, then supply low-carbon, and only then offset the small remainder. Reducing the carbon a scheme emits and embodies — through reuse, fabric, low-carbon materials and the measures above — always comes before buying it away. Embodied carbon deserves real attention on larger schemes, and whole-life carbon assessment is becoming normal.

title: 10. How to actually secure it
paras: Ten: how to actually secure it. None of this matters unless it ends up in the permission, and the mechanisms are the ones planners use every day. The earlier you raise these measures, the cheaper they are to secure — so pre-application is where most of the value is won, before the design is fixed and viability set. A sound, evidenced local plan policy is the strongest foundation. Conditions secure many measures — the water standard, cycle parking, landscaping, E-V charging beyond the Regulations — provided each meets the six tests: necessary, relevant to planning, relevant to the development, enforceable, precise and reasonable. Larger or off-site measures — a bus contribution, off-site tree planting, a genuine carbon-offset payment, long-term B-N-G management — run through section 106. And design review, the National Model Design Code and local codes give the greening and movement measures real weight.

Two cautions close the loop. Keep it proportionate: the full checklist is for a major scheme; a householder application needs a light touch, and asking a single extension for a travel plan is the kind of overreach that discredits the whole agenda. And keep it viable and lawful: every measure lands in the same viability appraisal as affordable housing and infrastructure, and a condition that fails the six tests, or an energy ask with no policy behind it, will not survive an appeal. Used with judgement, these small levers are the part of climate action a planner can pull on every single application. And added across a career and a district, they amount to a great deal more than they look.

Now for the PlanRef view. Reduce first. Don't gold-plate. Offset last. An offset contribution has its place — funding real, local, additional and monitored reduction, such as tree planting or the retrofit of existing homes. But it's a last resort for the genuine remainder — not a line a developer buys to avoid designing properly. And a "carbon-neutral" badge resting on cheap, unverifiable offsets is worth exactly nothing. Apply the same scepticism you bring to biodiversity and nutrient credits: additional, secured, monitored, local — or discounted. And on the everyday scheme, a well-placed bus stop, a decent bike store, a shade tree and a reused building save real carbon at almost no cost, while the grand gesture with the green label often saves none. The small, honest measure beats the large, marketed one. That's a view — but after two decades of evidence, a confident one.

title: Practice points
paras: And so to the practice points. First: small, cumulative, and yours. The everyday measures — the bike store, the bus stop, the shade tree, the reused building — are the part of climate action a development-management planner touches on every application. They add up.

Second: know the Building Regs boundary. Energy, E-V charging under Part S, overheating under Part O and, from the 24th of March 2027, the Future Homes Standard — seventy-five per cent less carbon, no gas, and solar — are Regs matters. Don't duplicate or gold-plate them case by case.

Third: but local energy policy survives. The Court of Appeal, in July 2025, confirmed that local planning authorities keep the power to set higher local energy standards where justified. Evidenced policy — not case-by-case invention.

Fourth: movement is the biggest lever. Make the walk, the cycle and the bus the easy choice, through layout, safe routes, a real bus stop and a bike store by the door. It locks in travel behaviour for sixty years.
Fifth: water counts. The hundred-and-ten-litres-per-person-per-day optional standard, by condition, where justified — and SuDS and permeable surfaces that manage flood and heat together.
Sixth: design waste storage in. Accessible, adequate recycling and food-waste storage lifts recycling for the life of the scheme; hard-to-reach bins get ignored.
Seventh: green it — and maintain it. Shade trees, hedgerows and a well-delivered ten per cent biodiversity net gain. The failure is follow-through: secure the maintenance, not just the plan.
Eighth: reduce first, offset last. Cut and reuse before you buy carbon away. Treat offsets like credits — additional, secured, monitored, local — or worth nothing.
And ninth: secure it early, and proportionately. Pre-app is where the value is won. Use policy, six-tests conditions and section 106. Scale the ask to the development, and keep it viable and lawful.

A note on the sources behind all this. The Future Homes and Buildings Standards, and the Building Regulations etcetera (Amendment) (England) Regulations 2026 — the Government response of March 2026, in force from the 24th of March 2027. That's the seventy-five-per-cent-less-carbon standard, the end of fossil-fuel heating, and mandatory solar for new homes.
Building Regulations Approved Document S — E-V charging, 2022 — and Approved Document O — overheating, 2022. Those set out what the Regulations, not planning, now require in new homes.

R, on the application of Rights: Community: Action, against the Secretary of State, reported at 2025, E-W-C-A Civ 990 — the Court of Appeal, July 2025 — together with the Planning and Energy Act 2008 and the Written Ministerial Statement of December 2023. That's the authority that local planning authorities retain the power to set higher local energy standards where justified.

The optional technical housing standard for water efficiency — a hundred and ten litres per person per day — the tighter standard an authority can require by condition where its local plan justifies it.
Biodiversity net gain — mandatory at ten per cent, for major development from February 2024 and for small sites from April 2024.

And then Active Travel England, a statutory consultee for larger applications since 2023; the Manual for Streets; L-T-N 1 of 20, Cycle Infrastructure Design; the National Model Design Code; and Simpler Recycling.

One last word. This was written as at the 21st of July 2026, and it covers England. Factual points on the Building Regulations, standards and law are attributed to their sources; the PlanRef view box is clearly-marked opinion. Standards and thresholds change — so re-check before relying on any point on a live application. And keep the ask proportionate, viable and lawful.
